summary: Joel Spolsky argues that the recording industry's push for variable pricing on iTunes isn't about charging fair market prices for popular vs. unpopular songs, but about gaining a manipulation tool. He draws an analogy to movie theaters, which charge uniform prices because lower prices would signal that a movie is bad, reducing attendance further. The recording industry wants variable pricing so they can use high prices to artificially signal quality for songs they want to promote, regardless of actual merit. This would also give labels leverage over artists by threatening to release singles at the low price tier, effectively killing them. Spolsky frames the iTunes pricing debate as a power struggle between Apple and the recording labels over who gets to influence consumer purchasing decisions.
key_points: Movie theaters charge uniform prices because differential pricing would signal quality judgments that hurt sales of cheaper-priced films
The recording industry wants variable pricing not to discount old songs, but to use price as a signal to manipulate which songs consumers perceive as valuable
Labels would price new releases high regardless of quality to fake a signal of worth, while using the low tier as a punishment category
Variable pricing would give labels leverage over artists by threatening to release singles into the cheap tier, killing them commercially
The real battle is between Apple and the recording industry over who controls the signaling mechanism that influences purchasing behavior
money_quotes: The answer is that pricing sends a signal. People have come to believe that "you get what you pay for."
If you lowered the price of a movie, people would immediately infer from the low price that it's a crappy movie and they wouldn't go see it.
The entertainment industry has to maintain a straight face and tell you that Gigli or Battlefield Earth are every bit as valuable as Wedding Crashers or Star Wars or nobody will go see them.

The reason the music recording industry wants different prices has nothing to do with making a premium on the best songs. What they really want is a system they can manipulate to send signals about what songs are worth.

When really bad songs come out, as long as they're new and the recording industry wants to promote those songs, they'll charge the full $2.49 or whatever it is to send a fake signal that the songs are better than they really are.
And now when a musician gets uppity, all the recording industry has to do is threaten to release their next single straight into the $0.99 category, which will kill it dead no matter how good it is.

In the battle between Apple and the recording industry over who gets to manipulate what songs you buy, Apple (like movie theaters) is going to be in favor of fixed prices, while the recording industry is going to want variable prices.
key_insight: Variable pricing in entertainment isn't about supply and demand—it's a signaling tool that whoever controls it uses to manipulate consumer perception of quality and gain leverage over content creators.
